title: Dog Ate Garden Fertilizer Pellets: Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ate fertilizer pellets, understand the risks, the medium urgency level, and why you should take a photo of the packaging for your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Fertilizer ingestion in dogs is a medium-urgency situation that often causes gastrointestinal upset but can lead to severe toxicity depending on the ingredients. Contact your veterinarian immediately and have a photo of the product label ready for professional assessment.

What happens when my dog eats garden fertilizer?

Garden fertilizer pellets are concentrated blends of minerals like nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, often mixed with organic materials like bone meal or blood meal. While the minerals themselves are often only mildly irritating, the additives or large quantities can cause significant gastrointestinal distress or toxicity in dogs.

Is fertilizer ingestion in dogs a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for fertilizer ingestion is generally Medium.
  • While most cases result in mild vomiting or diarrhea, some products contain dangerous additives like iron, herbicides, or pesticides.
  • Because it is difficult to determine the exact toxicity level without professional analysis, you should contact your veterinarian or a pet poison helpline immediately for guidance.

How does a photo of the fertilizer packaging help my veterinarian?

  • Taking a clear photo of the fertilizer bag, specifically the ingredients list and the N-P-K (nitrogen-phosphorus-potassium) ratio, is one of the most helpful steps you can take.
  • This allows a veterinary professional to quickly identify if there are hazardous additives, such as iron or systemic insecticides.
  • Accurate identification of ingredients ensures your pet receives the specific medical interventions or treatments required for their exposure.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following ingestion of garden fertilizer pellets by a dog, triage should include assessing the amount of elemental iron ingested, as ingestion at >20 mg/kg generally warrants decontamination via emesis and GI protectants. Activated charcoal is not effective for binding iron. For ingestions >60 mg/kg, additional treatment and monitoring are necessary. Milk of magnesia (magnesium hydroxide; 5-30 mL once or twice daily) can be administered to complex with iron and decrease its absorption. Serum iron levels and total serum iron binding capacity should be checked at 3 hours and again at 8-10 hours post-exposure. Chelation therapy may be considered if serum iron is >300 mcg/dL along with clinical signs (e.g., repeated vomiting, shock) or exceeds the total iron binding capacity. Be aware that clinical signs such as vomiting (with or without blood), hypovolemic shock, depression, fever, acidosis, and liver failure may occur 12-24 hours later, potentially following a period of apparent recovery; oliguria and anuria secondary to shock-induced renal failure may also occur.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: What symptoms should I watch for?
faqs.answer: Common signs include drooling, vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. In more severe cases involving iron toxicity, you may see lethargy or tremors.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically directed by a veterinarian. Some fertilizer components can be caustic or cause secondary issues if brought back up incorrectly.

faqs.question: Are organic fertilizers safer for dogs?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Organic fertilizers often contain bone meal or blood meal, which are very attractive to dogs and can cause them to eat large quantities, leading to potential stomach blockages or pancreatitis.
